note_physics_2_6_0.js.title: Common Misconception: Direction of Current
note_physics_2_6_0.js.text: Conventional current (+→−) and electron flow (−→+) are opposite directions. Conventional current is the standard used in circuit diagrams, but always remember the actual charge carriers (electrons) move opposite to the arrow direction.

note_physics_2_6_0.js.title: Exam Tip
note_physics_2_6_0.js.text: In circuit diagrams, always draw current arrows in the direction of conventional current (+ to −), not electron flow.

note_physics_2_6_1.js.title: Common Misconception: Voltage is Not Consumed
note_physics_2_6_1.js.text: Voltage measures energy per coulomb, not a quantity that gets 'used up' by components. Each coulomb of charge experiences the potential difference, but charge is not consumed.

note_physics_2_6_1.js.title: Measuring Voltage: Exam Tip
note_physics_2_6_1.js.text: Voltmeters must be connected in parallel, not series like ammeters. A voltmeter has very high resistance to draw minimal current from the circuit.

note_physics_2_6_2.js.title: Ohm's Law Only for Ohmic Conductors
note_physics_2_6_2.js.text: V = IR only applies to ohmic conductors at constant temperature. Filament lamps and diodes are non-ohmic — their resistance changes with voltage/current, producing curved I-V graphs rather than straight lines.

note_physics_2_6_2.js.title: Exam Tip: Reading I-V Graphs
note_physics_2_6_2.js.text: Resistance R = V/I at any point on the graph (V/I is the slope to that point, not the gradient of the line). For a straight line through the origin, the slope gives resistance directly.

note_physics_2_6_3.js.title: Current is Not Consumed
note_physics_2_6_3.js.text: Charge flows through components but is not 'used up' — the same current enters and leaves. At junctions, the sum of currents entering equals the sum leaving (charge conservation).

note_physics_2_6_3.js.title: Kirchhoff's First Law
note_physics_2_6_3.js.text: At any junction in a circuit, ΣI_in = ΣI_out. This applies regardless of whether the junction is a series or parallel arrangement.

note_physics_2_6_4.js.title: Kirchhoff's Second Law
note_physics_2_6_4.js.text: Around any closed loop, the algebraic sum of EMF = sum of potential differences across resistors (ΣE = ΣIR). This reflects energy conservation: the energy supplied by the source equals the energy dissipated in the circuit.

note_physics_2_6_4.js.title: Sign Convention for KVL
note_physics_2_6_4.js.text: When traversing a loop, assign positive direction arbitrarily. EMF and resistor PD with the same direction as traverse are positive; opposite direction are negative. If you have an error, the negative result tells you current flows backwards.

note_physics_2_6_5.js.title: Series and Parallel Resistances
note_physics_2_6_5.js.text: Series: R_total = R₁ + R₂ + ... (current same through all, voltage divides). Parallel: 1/R_total = 1/R₁ + 1/R₂ + ... (voltage same across all, current divides). A parallel resistor always reduces total resistance.

note_physics_2_6_5.js.title: Exam Tip: Power in Combinations
note_physics_2_6_5.js.text: In series: P ∝ R (highest power in largest resistor). In parallel: P ∝ 1/R (highest power in smallest resistor). Recognize these patterns to check work quickly.

note_physics_2_6_6.js.title: Three Power Formulas
note_physics_2_6_6.js.text: P = IV (most general); P = I²R (for resistive losses); P = V²/R (for voltage-limited supplies). Choose based on what you know. Check units always produce watts (J/s).

note_physics_2_6_6.js.title: Exam Tip: Selecting the Right Formula
note_physics_2_6_6.js.text: If you know current and resistance → P = I²R. If you know voltage and current → P = IV. If you know voltage and resistance → P = V²/R. Never guess — the correct formula depends on which quantities are given.

note_physics_2_6_7.js.title: Resistance ≠ Gradient of I-V Graph
note_physics_2_6_7.js.text: The gradient represents conductance (1/R), not resistance. Resistance is R = V/I at any point (ratio of any voltage to corresponding current), which is the slope from the origin to that point, not the slope of a tangent to the curve.

note_physics_2_6_7.js.title: Reading I-V Characteristics
note_physics_2_6_7.js.text: Ohmic (straight line through origin): constant R. Filament lamp (curve bending right): R increases with T. Diode (threshold then linear): R ≈∞ below threshold, then R ≈ small value above threshold.

note_physics_2_7_0.js.title: Resistivity Formula
note_physics_2_7_0.js.text: R = ρL/A: a longer wire has more resistance (R ∝ L), while a thicker wire has less (R ∝ 1/A). Resistivity ρ depends only on the material and temperature, not on the wire's dimensions.

note_physics_2_7_0.js.title: Exam Tip: Unit Check
note_physics_2_7_0.js.text: ρ is in Ω·m, L in m, A in m². Check: (Ω·m × m) / m² = Ω ✓. A common error is forgetting to square the radius when converting diameter to area.

note_physics_2_7_1.js.title: Core Practical: Measure diameter many times
note_physics_2_7_1.js.text: To find the wire diameter, measure at least 5–10 positions along the wire using a micrometer. Take the mean to reduce random error. A single measurement of diameter introduces large systematic error into R = ρL/A because resistance depends on A = πd²/4 (error in d is squared in the error for A).

note_physics_2_7_1.js.title: Avoid Heating the Wire
note_physics_2_7_1.js.text: The wire's resistance changes with temperature: ΔR/ΔT ≈ α × R₀. Heating from the current measurement itself causes R to drift, throwing off the experiment. Use moderate current and record readings quickly.

note_physics_2_7_2.js.title: Drift Velocity vs Signal Speed Paradox
note_physics_2_7_2.js.text: Electrons drift ~mm/s (extremely slowly) but the electric field propagates at ~c. The light comes on almost instantly when you flip a switch, not because electrons travel that fast, but because the field change/signal travels at near-light speed while electron motion is a separate effect.

note_physics_2_7_2.js.title: Remember I = nAev_d
note_physics_2_7_2.js.text: Current depends on drift velocity, cross-sectional area, and charge carrier density. A larger cross-section (bigger A) can carry more current even at the same drift velocity. Copper has higher charge carrier density (n) than other conductors, so copper wires can carry large currents.

note_physics_2_7_3.js.title: Potentiometer Principle
note_physics_2_7_3.js.text: For a uniform wire, V/L = constant = k (voltage per unit length). If you want to compare two EMFs using the null-deflection method, connect them to opposite ends of the potentiometer wire and find where the galvanometer reads zero — at that position, E₁/L₁ = E₂/L₂.

note_physics_2_7_3.js.title: Exam Tip: Why Null-Deflection is Accurate
note_physics_2_7_3.js.text: The potentiometer method measures EMF without drawing current (galvanometer at balance shows zero current), eliminating the error from internal resistance. This is more accurate than a direct voltmeter measurement.

note_physics_2_7_4.js.title: Loading Effect in Potential Dividers
note_physics_2_7_4.js.text: When you connect a load resistor R_L across the output of a potential divider, the effective output resistance drops and the output voltage falls below the no-load value V_out = V_in × R₂/(R₁+R₂). The lower the load resistance, the greater the voltage drop. This is critical in practical applications like sensor circuits.

note_physics_2_7_4.js.title: Exam Tip: Recognizing Loading
note_physics_2_7_4.js.text: If an exam question asks 'what happens when a load is connected?', state: (1) effective output resistance decreases, (2) output voltage decreases, (3) output current increases, (4) load current depends on load resistance.

note_physics_2_7_5.js.title: LDR and Thermistor Applications
note_physics_2_7_5.js.text: LDR (light-dependent resistor): resistance decreases with increasing light intensity. Thermistor (NTC): resistance decreases with increasing temperature. Both are used in sensor circuits where a potential divider converts resistance change into a voltage change that can be measured or used to trigger logic.

note_physics_2_7_5.js.title: Unit Conversion Trap
note_physics_2_7_5.js.text: Diameter must be converted to radius for area calculations: A = π(d/2)² = πr². Common error: using diameter directly gives A = πd² which is 4× too large. Also convert mm or cm to m for SI units: mm = 10⁻³ m, not 10⁻² m.

note_physics_2_8_0.js.title: EMF is NOT a Force
note_physics_2_8_0.js.text: EMF stands for electromotive force (energy per unit charge), NOT a mechanical force. It has units J/C (or V). The name is historical; EMF is really a measure of energy transfer, not force (N). An ideal voltmeter with infinite resistance reads the open-circuit EMF; a real voltmeter draws tiny current and reads slightly lower due to internal resistance.

note_physics_2_8_0.js.title: EMF vs Potential Difference
note_physics_2_8_0.js.text: EMF ε is the total energy per coulomb supplied by a source. Terminal potential difference V is the energy per coulomb available to the external circuit. The difference is lost inside the battery (I²r heating in the internal resistance).

note_physics_2_8_1.js.title: Internal Resistance Effect
note_physics_2_8_1.js.text: A real battery has internal resistance r. When current flows, the terminal PD V = ε − Ir is less than the EMF. From a V-I graph: y-intercept = ε (open-circuit EMF), gradient = −r (negative slope gives internal resistance).

note_physics_2_8_1.js.title: Short-Circuit Hazard
note_physics_2_8_1.js.text: If a battery is short-circuited (R = 0), the current I = ε/r becomes very large, causing rapid heating and potential explosion. This is why batteries must never be short-circuited, and why internal resistance limits the short-circuit current in a real battery.

note_physics_2_8_2.js.title: EMF ≠ Terminal PD When Current Flows
note_physics_2_8_2.js.text: Terminal potential difference V = ε − Ir is always less than EMF when current flows (lost volts = Ir). As current increases, terminal PD decreases linearly. A 1.5 V cell on open circuit has ε = 1.5 V, but when drawing current it shows V < 1.5 V.

note_physics_2_8_2.js.title: Exam Tip: Calculating Lost Volts
note_physics_2_8_2.js.text: Lost volts = Ir = I × internal resistance. This energy is dissipated as heat inside the battery (wasted as thermal energy). The external circuit receives energy at rate P = IV = I(ε − Ir) = Iε − I²r.

note_physics_2_8_3.js.title: Battery Heating During Measurement
note_physics_2_8_3.js.text: Repeated ammeter measurements heat the battery (P = I²r in internal resistance), raising its temperature and changing the internal resistance. Work quickly and allow cooling time between measurements if precision is needed. A voltmeter draws small current, causing slight underestimate of EMF, but this is usually negligible compared to other errors.

note_physics_2_8_3.js.title: Core Practical: Extraction Method
note_physics_2_8_3.js.text: Plot V vs I on a graph. Gradient = −r (internal resistance). Y-intercept = ε (EMF). This method extracts both ε and r from a single set of measurements, reducing systematic error compared to calculating individual values.

note_physics_2_8_4.js.title: Resistance vs Temperature
note_physics_2_8_4.js.text: Metals: R increases with T (lattice vibrations scatter electrons). Thermistor (NTC ceramic): R decreases sharply with T (more charge carriers become available). Superconductor: R → 0 below critical temperature T_c. Temperature coefficient α = ΔR/(R₀ΔT) is material-dependent.

note_physics_2_8_4.js.title: Exam Tip: Which Way Does R Change?
note_physics_2_8_4.js.text: Metals heat up → R increases → led becomes hotter (positive feedback). NTC thermistor heats up → R decreases → I increases (more heating). These opposite behaviors lead to different circuit responses — remember which material does what.

note_physics_2_8_5.js.title: Light-Dependent Resistor (LDR)
note_physics_2_8_5.js.text: Resistance decreases with light intensity (photons create charge carriers). In darkness R is very high (MΩ range); in bright light R drops to ~100 Ω. Used in light-sensor circuits, automatic brightness control, and alarm systems.

note_physics_2_8_5.js.title: Photosensitive Device Applications
note_physics_2_8_5.js.text: An LDR in a potential divider circuit produces a voltage that varies with light level. This voltage change can trigger logic circuits or be used to regulate a system automatically (e.g., street lights that activate at dusk).

note_physics_2_11_0.js.title: Wave Properties: Five Key Definitions
note_physics_2_11_0.js.text: Frequency f (Hz) is cycles per second. Period T (s) relates to frequency: T = 1/f. Wavelength λ (m) is distance between identical points (crest to crest). Amplitude A (m) is maximum displacement. Wave speed c (m/s) is distance traveled per second — NOT the same as particle velocity.

note_physics_2_11_0.js.title: Exam Tip: Unit Conversion for Frequency
note_physics_2_11_0.js.text: Always convert period from milliseconds (ms) or microseconds (μs) to seconds (s) before calculating frequency: f = 1/T. A period of 2 ms = 2 × 10⁻³ s → f = 500 Hz. Missing unit conversion is a frequent exam penalty.

note_physics_2_11_1.js.title: Wave Equation Relationships
note_physics_2_11_1.js.text: v = fλ links wave speed, frequency, and wavelength. For constant wave speed: if f ↑ then λ ↓ (inverse relationship). This applies to all waves — light, sound, water, radio — in any medium.

note_physics_2_11_1.js.title: Unit Conversion in Wave Equation
note_physics_2_11_1.js.text: Convert all quantities to SI before substitution: frequency in Hz, wavelength in m, wave speed in m/s. A common error: 100 cm/s is NOT 100 m/s; it is 1 m/s. Missing this converts the answer by a factor of 100.

note_physics_2_11_2.js.title: Don't Assume Sinusoidal = Transverse
note_physics_2_11_2.js.text: Both transverse and longitudinal waves can produce sinusoidal displacement-distance graphs. The difference is the direction: transverse particles vibrate perpendicular to travel direction; longitudinal particles vibrate parallel to travel direction. Read the question carefully.

note_physics_2_11_2.js.title: Longitudinal Waves: Compressions and Rarefactions
note_physics_2_11_2.js.text: Longitudinal waves (sound, P-waves) consist of regions of high pressure (compressions) alternating with low pressure (rarefactions). Wavelength λ is the distance between one compression and the next compression. These waves cannot be polarized (no up-down or left-right vibration).

note_physics_2_11_3.js.title: Transverse Wave Definition
note_physics_2_11_3.js.text: Particles vibrate perpendicular to the direction of wave travel (energy transfer). Shows crests (peaks) and troughs (valleys). Examples: light, water waves, guitar strings. Transverse waves CAN be polarized by passing through a filter.

note_physics_2_11_3.js.title: Distinguishing Wave Types on Graphs
note_physics_2_11_3.js.text: Displacement-distance graph (snapshot): read amplitude A and wavelength λ directly. Displacement-time graph: read amplitude A and period T directly. Calculate frequency from f = 1/T. Both graphs look identical but measure different things—the axis label is your clue.

note_physics_2_11_4.js.title: Common Graph Measurement Mistakes
note_physics_2_11_4.js.text: Measuring amplitude as peak-to-trough distance gives 2A (wrong). Measuring period from zero-crossing rising to next zero-crossing falling gives T/2 (wrong). Always measure full cycle: crest-to-crest, trough-to-trough, OR rising-zero to next rising-zero.

note_physics_2_11_4.js.title: Phase Difference from Distance Graph
note_physics_2_11_4.js.text: Two points separated by distance d have phase difference φ = (2π/λ) × d radians. One full wavelength apart → φ = 2π (in phase). Half-wavelength apart → φ = π (antiphase, 180°). Quarter-wavelength apart → φ = π/2 (90° out of phase).

note_physics_2_12_0.js.title: Energy is NOT Destroyed in Destructive Interference
note_physics_2_12_0.js.text: When two waves cancel at a point (destructive interference), energy is not destroyed—it is redistributed to nearby constructive-interference regions. Total energy is conserved; cancellation is spatial, not energy loss.

note_physics_2_12_0.js.title: Path Difference Conditions
note_physics_2_12_0.js.text: Constructive interference: path difference = nλ (n = 0, 1, 2, ...) → crests meet crests → amplitude doubles. Destructive interference: path difference = (n + ½)λ → crests meet troughs → amplitude cancels. These conditions determine bright (I_max) or dark (I = 0) fringes.

note_physics_2_12_1.js.title: Coherence is Essential for Stable Fringes
note_physics_2_12_1.js.text: Two incoherent sources (like separate lamps) have random, time-varying phase differences (~10⁻⁸ s changes). The interference pattern shifts rapidly and appears only as uniform brightness. A laser or single source split into two paths maintains constant phase—essential for stable fringes.

note_physics_2_12_1.js.title: Phase Difference ↔ Path Difference Link
note_physics_2_12_1.js.text: Path difference of λ ↔ phase difference 360° (2π rad). Path difference λ/2 ↔ 180° (π rad). General: phase diff (°) = (path diff / λ) × 360°. Convert between these representations quickly for exam calculations.

note_physics_2_12_2.js.title: Stationary Waves Do NOT Transfer Energy
note_physics_2_12_2.js.text: A progressive wave carries energy along the direction of travel. A stationary wave stores energy in the oscillating medium between nodes but transfers zero net energy along the wire — it is a resonance phenomenon, not propagation.

note_physics_2_12_2.js.title: Nodes and Antinodes
note_physics_2_12_2.js.text: Nodes: points of zero vibration (always); antinodes: points of maximum vibration (always). In a standing wave, nodes and antinodes remain fixed in space (do not move left-right). This contrasts with a progressive wave where all particles vibrate with the same amplitude.

note_physics_2_12_3.js.title: Total Length vs Vibrating Length
note_physics_2_12_3.js.text: When calculating μ = m/L, use the total string length (or wire reel length). When calculating fundamental frequency f₁ = v/(2L), use the vibrating length (distance between fixed supports)—a common exam trap is using the wrong length in the wrong place.

note_physics_2_12_3.js.title: Fundamental Frequency Formula
note_physics_2_12_3.js.text: f₁ = (1/2L)√(T/μ). Increasing tension T → higher f₁. Increasing length L → lower f₁. Increasing linear density μ (thicker string) → lower f₁. These dependencies explain why tightening a guitar string raises pitch and why thick bass strings are lower than thin treble strings.

note_physics_2_12_4.js.title: Filament Lamp Cannot Produce Stable Fringes
note_physics_2_12_4.js.text: A filament lamp emits light from millions of independent atoms, each emitting brief random-phase wave trains. The phase difference changes every ~10⁻⁸ seconds, causing the interference pattern to shift too fast to see—only uniform brightness is observed. Use a laser instead.

note_physics_2_12_4.js.title: Core Practical 7: Identifying Harmonics
note_physics_2_12_4.js.text: Drive the string with an oscillator at varying frequency. When frequency matches a natural (harmonic) frequency, a stable standing-wave pattern appears. Fundamental shows 1 antinode (2 nodes); second harmonic shows 2 antinodes (3 nodes); nth harmonic shows n antinodes (n+1 nodes). Count the loops to identify the harmonic number.
